# Ground Truth: Vataxia

## Metadata
- **Date audited:** 2026-03-16
- **Audited by:** Auto-generated auditor
- **Skill focus:** Privacy by Design review - PII manifests, data flow analysis, default config audits, lifecycle management

## Must-Find Items

Critical findings any competent assessment should identify within this skill's focus area.

| # | Finding | Category | Expected Severity | Expected Confidence | Reasoning | Acceptable Alternatives |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|---------------------|-----------|------------------------|
| 1 | Hardcoded SECRET_KEY in version control at `config/settings/base.py:5` with value `'a1y425b-u7(1_j%ekoh+_v_d620p5l5zfl6+fq&vwd+suq^j*z'` | Secret Management / PbD Principle 7 (Proactive) | CRITICAL | HIGH | Django SECRET_KEY is used for cryptographic signing of sessions, tokens, and CSRF protection. Exposing it in version control allows attackers to forge sessions, bypass CSRF protection, and decrypt sensitive data. Violates principle of proactive security. | May note this is in base settings but still critical |
| 2 | Unrestricted ALLOWED_HOSTS = ['*'] in `config/settings/base.py:34` | Security Configuration / PbD Principle 1 (Proactive) | HIGH | HIGH | Allows Host header injection attacks and cache poisoning. Any domain can be used to access the application. Violates proactive security principle and defense-in-depth. | May categorize as MEDIUM if considering it's development/demo code |
| 3 | Private messages lack deletion mechanism at `v1/private_messages/views/private_message.py` - no DELETE endpoint | Data Minimization / PbD Principle 3 (Privacy by Default) | HIGH | HIGH | Users cannot delete their private messages (subject and body in `PrivateMessage` model), violating data minimization and user control principles. Messages persist indefinitely without user consent. | May note partial endpoint implementation |
| 4 | CORS_ORIGIN_ALLOW_ALL = True in `config/settings/base.py:94` allows any origin | Security Configuration / PbD Principle 1 (Proactive) | HIGH | HIGH | Any website can make authenticated requests to the API, enabling CSRF-like attacks and data exfiltration. Violates principle of proactive security and least privilege. | May rate as CRITICAL depending on context |
| 5 | PII fields (email, first_name, last_name) exposed in all User serializers at `v1/accounts/serializers/user.py` without granular access control | Data Minimization / PbD Principle 4 (Full Functionality) | MEDIUM | HIGH | User listing endpoint `GET /users` (`v1/accounts/views/user.py:21`) returns all users with full PII to any authenticated user. Violates data minimization - users don't need to see all other users' email addresses. | May categorize as HIGH severity |
| 6 | No data retention policy or automated deletion mechanisms for any PII-containing models | Data Lifecycle / PbD Principle 3 (Privacy by Default) | HIGH | HIGH | All user data, posts, messages, replies persist indefinitely with no retention limits. Violates data minimization and privacy-by-default principles. No mechanism for time-based data purging. | Could rate MEDIUM if considering typical practice |
| 7 | Missing on_delete cascade specifications for all ForeignKey fields in models | Data Lifecycle / PbD Principle 3 (Privacy by Default) | MEDIUM | HIGH | ForeignKeys in models (User, Post, PrivateMessage, etc.) don't specify on_delete behavior. In older Django versions, defaults to CASCADE which may cause unintended data retention or loss. Affects proper data lifecycle management. | May note Django version-specific behavior |
| 8 | Admin panel exposes all PII without field-level restrictions at `v1/accounts/admin.py` and `v1/private_messages/admin.py` | Access Control / PbD Principle 5 (End-to-End Security) | MEDIUM | HIGH | Django admin registers User, Profile, ResetPasswordCode, and PrivateMessage models with full field access. No read-only fields, no password hiding, no private message content protection. | May vary based on admin deployment assumptions |
| 9 | No audit logging for sensitive operations (user deletion, password resets, message access) | Visibility / PbD Principle 6 (Visibility and Transparency) | HIGH | HIGH | No logging of who accessed private messages, who deleted users, when passwords were reset. Impossible to detect privacy breaches or unauthorized access. Violates transparency principle. | Could rate MEDIUM based on context |
| 10 | Reset password codes never expire at `v1/accounts/models/reset_password_code.py` | Security / PbD Principle 1 (Proactive) | HIGH | HIGH | ResetPasswordCode model has no expiration timestamp. Codes remain valid indefinitely in database, increasing attack surface. Once obtained, attacker has unlimited time to use code. | None - this is clearly an issue |
| 11 | Profile images stored without access control or privacy settings at `v1/accounts/models/profile.py:6` | Data Protection / PbD Principle 4 (Full Functionality) | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | Profile images are ImageFields with no privacy controls. All profile images publicly accessible via media URLs. No option for private profiles or image visibility settings. | May note this depends on media serving configuration |
| 12 | Database credentials in version control at `config/settings/production.py:10-18` | Secret Management / PbD Principle 7 (Proactive) | CRITICAL | HIGH | Production database credentials (even if placeholders 'xxx') are in tracked file. Structure encourages committing real credentials. Should use environment variables. | May note these are placeholders but pattern is dangerous |
| 13 | No encryption for private message content in `v1/private_messages/models/private_message.py:9-10` | Data Protection / PbD Principle 5 (End-to-End Security) | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | Private message subject and body stored as plaintext CharField/TextField. Accessible to database administrators and anyone with database access. No end-to-end encryption option. | Reasonable to rate LOW-MEDIUM depending on threat model |
| 14 | Password reset uses UUID but code returned in GET response exposes timing/enumeration at `v1/accounts/views/reset_password.py` | Security / PbD Principle 1 (Proactive) | LOW | MEDIUM | While UUID provides good entropy, the password reset mechanism lacks rate limiting and the authentication_classes/permission_classes are empty tuples allowing anonymous access without abuse protection. | May focus on different aspects of this endpoint |
| 15 | User deletion allowed by moderators without data export or confirmation at `v1/accounts/views/user.py:68-87` | Data Subject Rights / PbD Principle 6 (Visibility and Transparency) | HIGH | HIGH | Moderators can delete users (line 86) but there's no data export mechanism, no user notification, no confirmation workflow. Violates right to data portability and transparency. | May rate MEDIUM depending on interpretation |

## Nice-to-Find Items

Deeper findings that demonstrate thoroughness.

| # | Finding | Category | Expected Severity | Expected Confidence | Reasoning |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|---------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Fixture files contain sample user data with hashed passwords at `v1/accounts/fixtures/user.json` | Data Leakage | LOW | HIGH | Repository contains fixture data with real (sample) PII: email addresses, names, hashed passwords for 3 users. While intentional for development, demonstrates poor separation of test/prod data practices. |
| 2 | No Content-Security-Policy, HSTS, or secure cookie settings in any configuration | Security Headers | MEDIUM | HIGH | Missing SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT, SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE, CSRF_COOKIE_SECURE, SECURE_HSTS_SECONDS in all settings files. Sessions/cookies vulnerable to interception over HTTP. |
| 3 | CreatedModified mixin tracks modification dates but no audit trail of who modified at `v1/general/created_modified.py` | Audit Trail | MEDIUM | HIGH | Models track when records were created/modified but not by whom. Insufficient for compliance requirements (GDPR Article 30 records of processing). |
| 4 | Media files committed to repository at `/media/` directory | Data Practice | LOW | MEDIUM | Sample images (bucky.jpg, etc.) committed to git despite .gitignore entry for media/*. Demonstrates poor separation of code and user data. |
| 5 | No rate limiting on authentication endpoints (login, password reset) | Abuse Prevention | MEDIUM | HIGH | `v1/accounts/views/login.py` and `reset_password.py` have no throttling configured. Vulnerable to credential stuffing and brute force attacks. No DRF throttle classes in REST_FRAMEWORK settings. |
| 6 | Default permission class IsAuthenticatedOrReadOnly allows public read access at `config/settings/base.py:61` | Access Control | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | Default REST_FRAMEWORK permission allows unauthenticated users to read all endpoints except those explicitly protected. Posts, user profiles, etc. may be publicly readable. |
| 7 | Invitation codes are UUIDs but never invalidated/expired at `v1/credits/models/invitation.py` | Lifecycle Management | MEDIUM | HIGH | Invitation.code is UUID4 with good entropy, but invitations remain valid forever once created. No expiration mechanism or limit on reuse attempts. |
| 8 | Wallet balance can go negative with no validation at `v1/credits/views/invitation.py:36` and `transfer.py:37` | Business Logic | LOW | LOW | Wallet.balance can become negative during invitation creation or transfers. While not strictly privacy, affects data integrity and user trust. |
| 9 | Environment configuration committed to repository at `config/environment.py` | Configuration Management | LOW | MEDIUM | Environment selection (local/development/production) committed to git. Encourages direct editing rather than environment-based configuration. |
| 10 | No IP address logging or geolocation tracking for security events | Security Monitoring | MEDIUM | LOW | No recording of IP addresses for failed logins, password changes, or access to sensitive data. Makes incident response and threat detection difficult. Note: Could also be viewed positively as data minimization. |

## Known Ambiguities

Findings where reasonable reviewers might disagree.

| Finding | Why It's Ambiguous | Acceptable Interpretations |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| Django admin panel PII exposure | Admin is typically restricted to superusers only; some argue this is acceptable | **Strict interpretation**: Admin should still have field-level controls and read-only sensitive fields. **Lenient interpretation**: Admin access implies full trust, no additional controls needed. |
| Profile image privacy | Depends on whether this is intended as a public social network | **Public platform interpretation**: Profile images should be public by default (LOW severity). **Privacy-first interpretation**: Users should control image visibility (MEDIUM-HIGH severity). |
| Private message plaintext storage | End-to-end encryption isn't standard practice for most platforms | **High security interpretation**: Private messages should be encrypted at rest (HIGH severity). **Pragmatic interpretation**: Database-level encryption is sufficient for most use cases (LOW severity). |
| No encryption at rest for any database fields | Django doesn't provide this out of box; often handled at infrastructure level | **Application-level interpretation**: Critical PII should use field-level encryption libraries (MEDIUM severity). **Infrastructure interpretation**: Database/disk encryption is sufficient (LOW/NONE severity). |
| Lack of data export functionality | GDPR right to data portability | **Compliance interpretation**: Missing data export is HIGH severity violation of GDPR Article 20. **Practical interpretation**: Can be implemented via admin actions or separate tooling (MEDIUM severity). |
| Public read access to user list | Social network may intentionally show all users | **Privacy interpretation**: User directory should be opt-in only (HIGH severity). **Feature interpretation**: This is intentional design for a social platform (LOW severity). |
| No email verification on signup | Depends on invitation-based vs. open registration model | **Security interpretation**: Email should be verified to prevent abuse (MEDIUM severity). **Design interpretation**: Invitation system provides verification (LOW severity). |

## Red Herrings

Things that look like issues within this skill's focus area but are not.

| Item | Why It Looks Like an Issue | Why It's Not |
|------|--------------------------|-------------|
| Password field in UserSerializerCreate at `v1/accounts/serializers/user.py:44` | Password appears in serializer fields list, suggesting it might be returned in responses | The serializer only uses this for input (create/update). Django's set_password() method (line 33 of views/user.py) properly hashes the password. Password is never returned in responses (not in UserSerializer fields). |
| Token returned in UserSerializerLogin at `v1/accounts/serializers/user.py:74` | Exposing auth tokens might seem like a security issue | This is intentional and correct behavior for token-based authentication. The token is meant to be shared with the client for subsequent requests. This is standard DRF TokenAuthentication pattern. |
| Debug toolbar in INSTALLED_APPS at `config/settings/development.py:17` | Debug toolbar can expose sensitive information | It's only in development.py, not production.py, and is conditioned on DEBUG=True. This is appropriate separation of concerns. The gitignore even excludes these environment files. |
| Missing GDPR consent tracking | No explicit consent model or privacy policy acceptance | This appears to be a sample/educational project (open source social network demo). GDPR consent requirements depend on deployment context and aren't always application-level concerns. |
| .gitignore excludes migrations at line 8 | Ignoring migrations could lead to database inconsistencies | This is actually intentional for this project structure - migrations are generated per-environment. The `__init__.py` is preserved (line 9) to keep the migrations module. Common pattern for some Django projects. |
| User.password field visible in admin | The password field is editable in UserAdmin | Django admin automatically displays password fields as read-only password hash widgets. The exclude in UserAdmin (line 8) only removes groups/permissions. Passwords are still protected by Django's built-in admin behavior. |
| auto_now and auto_now_add in CreatedModified | Automatic timestamps might not reflect actual user actions | These fields are explicitly for system-level tracking of record lifecycle, not user actions. This is correct implementation of audit trail metadata. Not intended to track user-initiated changes. |
| Files with `authentication_classes = ()` and `permission_classes = ()` | Empty tuples look like authentication bypass | This is intentional for public endpoints like login, password reset, and invitation acceptance. These endpoints must be accessible without authentication by design. |
| Database credentials as 'xxx' in production.py | Placeholder credentials in production settings | These are clearly placeholders (repeated 'xxx'). The file is also in .gitignore (line 14). This is a template pattern, not actual credentials. Still noted in Must-Find #12 as a pattern issue. |
